stem: Carlson v. Green held Bivens applied to an Eighth Amendment medical-care claim by a federal inmate, even though the FTCA provided a remedy. The Court reasoned:
opt_a: FTCA's exclusive-remedy provisions had not yet been enacted
opt_b: FTCA does not permit suits arising in federal prisons
opt_c: Bivens damages directly against officers serve different functions than FTCA recovery (governed by state tort law)
opt_d: The plaintiff was suing for injunctive relief that the FTCA does not authorize
explanation: Carlson: Bivens and FTCA serve different purposes. Bivens provides uniform federal damages directly against officers; FTCA depends on state tort law.

stem: Cannon v. University of Chicago held Title IX includes an implied private right of action for:
opt_a: Sex discrimination only where federal funds directly subsidize the discriminatory program
opt_b: Disparate-impact sex discrimination, given the regulations
opt_c: Intentional sex discrimination, given the rights-creating language and Congressional intent
opt_d: Both intentional and disparate-impact claims, broadly construed
explanation: Cannon: Title IX implies PROA for intentional sex discrimination. Sandoval later cabined the PROA to intentional claims.

stem: Alabama administers driver's license tests only in English. A non-English-speaking applicant sues, alleging this disparately impacts national-origin minorities under Title VI's regulations. After Sandoval:
opt_a: Private parties may sue if the agency promulgating the regulations expressly authorized
opt_b: The applicant's exclusive remedy is administrative complaint to DOJ
opt_c: No private right of action exists for disparate-impact claims under Title VI's regulations
opt_d: Private parties may sue because Title VI's regulations clearly intend private enforcement
explanation: Sandoval: only Congress, not an agency through regulation, can create a private right of action; Title VI's text supports only intentional-discrimination PROAs.

stem: Cort v. Ash's four-factor test for implied private rights of action included:
opt_a: Express text, agency interpretation, history of enforcement, and congressional acquiescence
opt_b: Necessity, particularity, redressability, and substantial federal interest
opt_c: Beneficiary class, legislative intent, fit with statutory purpose, and traditional state-law area
opt_d: Standing, ripeness, mootness, and political-question avoidance
explanation: Cort v. Ash's classic four-factor analysis. Largely supplanted by Sandoval's text-and-structure approach.

stem: In Health & Hospital Corp. v. Talevski (2023), the Court held that Spending-Clause statutes can support § 1983 enforcement when:
opt_a: The agency administering the statute expressly authorizes private suits
opt_b: The state has accepted the federal funds at issue
opt_c: The text uses unambiguous rights-creating language and there is no comprehensive remedial scheme that displaces § 1983
opt_d: The statute creates a categorical entitlement to damages
explanation: Talevski: rights-creating language plus absence of an exclusive remedial scheme allows § 1983 enforcement. Spending-Clause origin is not categorically disqualifying.

stem: A U.S. Border Patrol agent shoots and kills a Mexican national across the border. The family sues under Bivens for Fourth and Fifth Amendment violations. After Hernandez v. Mesa:
opt_a: The family's exclusive remedy is diplomatic resolution through the State Department
opt_b: Bivens applies because the U.S. Constitution reaches federal officers' conduct extraterritorially
opt_c: No Bivens action lies because cross-border shootings present a new context with foreign-relations special factors
opt_d: Bivens applies for the Fourth Amendment but not the Fifth
explanation: Hernandez v. Mesa: cross-border context plus foreign-relations special factors defeat Bivens extension.

stem: Bush v. Lucas declined to recognize Bivens because:
opt_a: The civil-service scheme provided full damages equivalent to Bivens
opt_b: Federal employees implicitly waive Bivens by accepting government employment
opt_c: The federal civil-service remedial scheme provided adequate alternative review
opt_d: First Amendment retaliation is not a Bivens-cognizable harm
explanation: Bush v. Lucas: when Congress has crafted a comprehensive remedial scheme, the implication is that further remedies are not warranted.

stem: Ex parte Young permits federal-court suits against state officers for:
opt_a: State-law violations where federal jurisdiction is otherwise proper
opt_b: Both prospective and retroactive relief, with appropriate clear-statement abrogation
opt_c: Retroactive damages against the state treasury
opt_d: Prospective injunctive relief to halt ongoing constitutional violations
explanation: Ex parte Young's narrow lane: prospective relief only. Edelman bars retroactive damages; Pennhurst bars state-law enforcement.

stem: A plaintiff successfully sues a state official under Ex parte Young and obtains an injunction. The injunction has the practical effect of compelling the state to pay $5M in back wages. Under Edelman v. Jordan:
opt_a: The retroactive monetary aspect violates the 11th Amendment, even though styled as prospective compliance
opt_b: The injunction is permissible because it operates against the official, not the state directly
opt_c: The injunction is permissible only if the back wages exceed a de minimis threshold
opt_d: The injunction is permissible if § 1983 abrogates state immunity for back wages
explanation: Edelman v. Jordan: Ex parte Young permits prospective relief but bars retroactive monetary relief from the state treasury.

stem: A class of disabled residents sues to compel state mental-health facilities to comply with state-law standards governing institutional conditions. Under Pennhurst v. Halderman:
opt_a: Federal courts have inherent jurisdiction to ensure state compliance with state law
opt_b: The supremacy clause permits federal-court enforcement of state law in civil-rights cases
opt_c: No federal-court Ex parte Young suit lies because federal courts cannot use Young to enforce state law against state officials
opt_d: Federal courts may enforce state law against state officials whenever the underlying claim is constitutional
explanation: Pennhurst v. Halderman: Young is for federal-law enforcement; using it to enforce state law against state officials would intrude on state sovereignty.

stem: Under Seminole Tribe v. Florida, congressional abrogation of state sovereign immunity requires:
opt_a: An unequivocal expression of intent and a state's express consent
opt_b: Article I commerce-power authority and § 5 enforcement together
opt_c: A federal interest sufficient to displace state sovereign immunity
opt_d: An unequivocal expression of intent to abrogate AND exercise of a constitutional power that permits abrogation
explanation: Seminole Tribe's two-step. Article I commerce powers are insufficient; § 5 of the 14th Amendment works.

stem: An employee sues a state university under the Family and Medical Leave Act's family-care provision. Under Nevada Department of Human Resources v. Hibbs:
opt_a: The FMLA's family-care provision is a valid § 5 abrogation because it remedies historical sex discrimination in family leave
opt_b: The employee's exclusive remedy is administrative review under state employment law
opt_c: The FMLA cannot abrogate state immunity because it is grounded in commerce power
opt_d: The FMLA's abrogation is invalid because it lacks 'congruence and proportionality'
explanation: Hibbs: FMLA's family-care provision was upheld as valid § 5 abrogation, citing the history of sex discrimination Congress sought to remedy.

stem: A paraplegic plaintiff sues a state under Title II of the ADA, alleging the state's courthouse lacks accessibility. Under Tennessee v. Lane:
opt_a: The plaintiff's exclusive remedy is administrative complaint to DOJ
opt_b: Title II applies only to private accommodations, not state courthouses
opt_c: Title II's abrogation is invalid because it exceeds § 5 'congruence and proportionality'
opt_d: Title II's abrogation is valid as applied because access to courts implicates fundamental rights
explanation: Tennessee v. Lane: Title II abrogation is valid as applied to fundamental-rights cases like access to courts.

stem: Central Virginia Community College v. Katz upheld bankruptcy proceedings against state interests because:
opt_a: Congress validly abrogated SSI under § 5 of the 14th Amendment
opt_b: The 11th Amendment does not address Article I bankruptcy
opt_c: States consented to federal bankruptcy jurisdiction in the plan of the constitutional convention
opt_d: Bankruptcy is a public-rights matter outside state immunity
explanation: Katz: ratification of the Bankruptcy Clause implicitly waived state immunity for bankruptcy proceedings — the 'plan of convention' theory.

stem: Torres v. Texas Department of Public Safety extended the plan-of-convention reasoning to:
opt_a: Suits enforcing federal admiralty law against state actors
opt_b: Suits enforcing federal employment protections under congressional war powers
opt_c: Suits enforcing the Spending Clause as applied to states
opt_d: Suits enforcing the Equal Protection Clause directly
explanation: Torres held that states yielded sovereign immunity for war-power-based federal claims (USERRA) under the plan of convention.

stem: Monroe v. Pape held that § 1983 reaches:
opt_a: Federal officials acting jointly with state officials
opt_b: State officials acting under color of state law, even when violating state law
opt_c: Only state officials in their official capacity
opt_d: Only state officials acting within the scope of their authority
explanation: Monroe interpreted 'under color of' broadly to reach abuses of authority — the heart of § 1983 enforcement against rogue officers.

stem: Monell v. NYC Department of Social Services held that a municipality is liable under § 1983:
opt_a: Only in suits seeking injunctive relief
opt_b: Only when the deprivation arises from a policy or custom — not under respondeat superior
opt_c: Whenever the municipality fails to indemnify the offending employee
opt_d: Whenever an employee acts under color of municipal authority
explanation: Monell's foundational rule: municipalities are 'persons' under § 1983 but liable only for policy/custom-based violations.

stem: Under AEDPA § 2254(d)(1), federal habeas relief from a state-court adjudication on the merits requires that the state decision was:
opt_a: Subject to plain-error review under federal procedural standards
opt_b: Based on an interpretation of state law inconsistent with federal precedent
opt_c: Erroneous, regardless of whether reasonable jurists could disagree
opt_d: Contrary to or an unreasonable application of clearly established federal law
explanation: § 2254(d)(1) sets up a deferential 'contrary to / unreasonable application' standard, not de novo review.

stem: A state court applies Crawford v. Washington but reaches a result opposite the Supreme Court's on materially indistinguishable facts. Under § 2254(d)(1):
opt_a: The federal court must defer to the state-court factual interpretation
opt_b: The state-court decision involved an 'unreasonable application' but federal habeas relief still requires AEDPA deference
opt_c: The state-court interpretation of Crawford is binding on the federal court
opt_d: The state-court decision is 'contrary to' clearly established federal law and federal habeas relief is available
explanation: Williams v. Taylor: 'contrary to' captures cases where the state court reaches the opposite result on materially indistinguishable facts.

stem: A state court correctly identifies Strickland v. Washington as the governing rule for ineffective-assistance claims. The state court then applies Strickland in a way no fairminded jurist could agree with. Under § 2254(d)(1):
opt_a: The state-court decision is shielded under AEDPA's deferential standard
opt_b: The federal court must remand for state-court reconsideration
opt_c: The state-court decision was 'contrary to' clearly established federal law
opt_d: The state-court decision involved an 'unreasonable application' of clearly established federal law
explanation: Williams v. Taylor: 'unreasonable application' captures cases where the state court chose the right rule but applied it unreasonably.

stem: A federal habeas petitioner seeks to develop new evidence in federal court showing his trial counsel was ineffective. The state court adjudicated the IATC claim on the merits without permitting discovery. Under Cullen v. Pinholster:
opt_a: The federal habeas court must conduct an evidentiary hearing to ensure full and fair review
opt_b: New evidence is admissible only if the petitioner can show diligent state-court development
opt_c: § 2254(d)(1) review is limited to the record before the state court at the time of its merits adjudication
opt_d: § 2254(d)(2) factual review is also required before granting relief
explanation: Pinholster: when § 2254(d)(1) applies, federal review is limited to the state-court record. Discovery on AEDPA review is sharply curtailed.

stem: A defendant fails to object at trial to coerced-confession evidence; the state appellate court denies the claim under a state-procedural-default rule. The federal habeas court should:
opt_a: Apply AEDPA's § 2254(d)(1) standard to the procedural default itself
opt_b: Apply the cause-and-prejudice standard to determine whether to excuse the procedural default
opt_c: Defer to the state-court procedural ruling without further inquiry
opt_d: Reach the merits because federal courts review federal claims de novo
explanation: Wainwright v. Sykes: cause-and-prejudice (or actual-innocence gateway) is the standard for excusing procedural default.

stem: A federal habeas petitioner argues that his collateral-review counsel was ineffective for failing to raise an IATC claim. Under Coleman v. Thompson:
opt_a: Collateral-review attorney error excuses default if the underlying claim is colorable
opt_b: Collateral-review attorney error is generally not 'cause' to excuse default, with a narrow Martinez exception
opt_c: Collateral-review counsel's negligence triggers a Strickland-style federal habeas analysis
opt_d: Collateral-review attorney error is always 'cause' under federal due-process standards
explanation: Coleman: no constitutional right to collateral-review counsel means error there isn't 'cause' (Martinez carved a narrow exception).

stem: In an Arizona-style state where IATC claims must be raised first on initial-review collateral, the petitioner's collateral-review counsel was ineffective. Under Martinez v. Ryan:
opt_a: Ineffective collateral-review counsel is never 'cause' under Coleman v. Thompson
opt_b: The procedural default is automatically excused because no other forum was available
opt_c: Ineffective collateral-review counsel may serve as 'cause' to excuse default of the substantial IATC claim
opt_d: The petitioner must independently establish actual innocence to excuse default
explanation: Martinez v. Ryan: where state law channels IATC to initial-review collateral, ineffective counsel there can be 'cause' for the IATC default.

stem: After a state conviction becomes final, the U.S. Supreme Court announces a new constitutional rule of criminal procedure regarding jury selection. Under Teague v. Lane:
opt_a: The new rule applies on collateral review only to capital cases
opt_b: The new rule generally does not apply on collateral review unless it falls within the substantive-rule or watershed-procedural exceptions
opt_c: The new rule applies on collateral review because federal courts apply current constitutional law
opt_d: The new rule applies on collateral review only if the state expressly opts in
explanation: Teague: new procedural rules don't apply retroactively on collateral review except for narrow exceptions.

stem: A state prisoner whose Fourth Amendment exclusionary-rule claim was fully litigated in state court (suppression hearing, appellate review) seeks federal habeas relief. Under Stone v. Powell:
opt_a: Federal habeas review proceeds under § 2254(d)(1) deference
opt_b: Federal habeas review proceeds de novo because 4A claims are core
opt_c: Federal habeas relief is available only if the petitioner is actually innocent
opt_d: Federal habeas relief is unavailable because the state provided full and fair litigation opportunity
explanation: Stone v. Powell: 4A exclusionary-rule claims are not cognizable on habeas where the state gave full and fair litigation opportunity.

stem: A petitioner files federal habeas more than one year after conviction-finality but produces credible new affidavits showing actual innocence. Under McQuiggin v. Perkins:
opt_a: The actual-innocence showing equitably tolls AEDPA's one-year statute of limitations
opt_b: The petition must be dismissed because AEDPA's SOL is jurisdictional
opt_c: The petitioner must produce DNA evidence to invoke the actual-innocence gateway
opt_d: Actual innocence excuses procedural default but not the AEDPA SOL
explanation: McQuiggin: a credible actual-innocence claim under Schlup serves as an equitable gateway through AEDPA's SOL.

stem: After winning at the trial court but losing on appeal, plaintiff settles the case before Supreme Court review. Under the Munsingwear doctrine, the appellate court should:
opt_a: Dismiss the appeal but leave the lower-court ruling intact
opt_b: Vacate the lower-court decision because the case is now moot through no fault of the petitioner
opt_c: Refer the case to the lower court for entry of judgment by stipulation
opt_d: Affirm the lower-court decision because the parties have resolved the dispute
explanation: United States v. Munsingwear: when mootness arises from happenstance (settlement, etc.), the appellate court vacates the lower judgment to prevent it from having preclusive effect.

stem: An organization sues for itself, alleging the challenged government policy diverts resources it would otherwise use elsewhere and frustrates its mission. Under modern doctrine after Havens Realty:
opt_a: Organizational standing exists if the plaintiff has suffered a concrete drain on its resources directly traceable to the policy
opt_b: Organizational standing is categorical for any 501(c)(3) advocacy organization
opt_c: Organizational standing requires Congressional designation of the organization
opt_d: Organizational standing requires identifying specific injured members under associational-standing rules
explanation: Havens Realty: an organization may have standing in its own right when it has suffered a concrete drain on resources caused by the challenged conduct (distinct from associational standing on behalf of members).

stem: A class action challenging an unconstitutional state policy survives mootness if:
opt_a: The dispute falls within the public-interest mootness exception
opt_b: Congress has expressly authorized continued judicial review
opt_c: All class members can show ongoing standing in their own right
opt_d: Class certification relates back to the named plaintiff's live claim, even after the named plaintiff's mootness
explanation: Sosna v. Iowa: relation-back doctrine permits class actions to survive named-plaintiff mootness when certification is appropriate and the class includes ongoing-controversy members.

stem: Plaintiff alleges injury from a federal regulation but the harm is shared in roughly equal measure with the broader public. The standing analysis:
opt_a: Treats the harm as a generalized grievance, generally insufficient for Article III standing
opt_b: Permits standing if the plaintiff is in the regulation's zone of interests
opt_c: Requires Congressional authorization to confer standing
opt_d: Permits standing because the regulation is a public-rights matter
explanation: Generalized grievances shared widely with the public lack the particularization Article III requires (Richardson, Schlesinger). Distinct from concrete injuries that happen to be shared by many similarly situated.

stem: A trade association challenges a federal regulation in its 'as applied' phase, arguing that the regulation will harm specific members in specific ways. Under modern ripeness doctrine:
opt_a: Ripeness requires actual enforcement against an identified member
opt_b: The case may be ripe even before enforcement if specific members face concrete compliance burdens
opt_c: Ripeness depends on whether the regulation has gone through notice-and-comment
opt_d: Ripeness requires a facial challenge, not as-applied
explanation: Abbott Labs's two-prong test (fitness + hardship) governs as-applied pre-enforcement challenges; concrete compliance burdens satisfy hardship.

stem: Two parties settle a tort case but agree to file a 'collusive' lawsuit in federal court for a declaratory judgment about the underlying contract issue. Under Muskrat v. United States and modern doctrine:
opt_a: The case is justiciable if a federal-law issue is presented
opt_b: The case is justiciable because both parties consent to federal jurisdiction
opt_c: The case is justiciable only if the federal court certifies the question to the state
opt_d: The case is nonjusticiable because there is no genuine adversity between the parties
explanation: Muskrat / Marbury: collusive suits without adversity violate Article III's case-or-controversy requirement, regardless of the parties' consent.

stem: A First Amendment plaintiff challenges a state's criminal false-statement law, alleging she intends to make statements that arguably fall within its proscriptive reach. The state has not yet prosecuted but has prosecuted others under the same law. Under SBA List v. Driehaus:
opt_a: The case is ripe only if the plaintiff has been investigated by the state
opt_b: The case is ripe because of the credible threat of enforcement plus chilling of protected speech
opt_c: The case is unripe because the plaintiff has not herself been prosecuted
opt_d: The case is unripe absent a definitive enforcement threat from the prosecutor
explanation: SBA List: pre-enforcement First Amendment challenges are ripe with credible threat plus chilling effect on protected conduct, even without prior personal prosecution.

stem: Plaintiff alleges the federal government's denial of a license has caused her concrete economic harm. The license-denial standard requires the agency to balance competing public interests. The standing analysis:
opt_a: Treats the alleged harm as concrete and particularized, but the agency's discretionary balancing affects the merits not standing
opt_b: Conditions standing on plaintiff's compliance with administrative-exhaustion requirements
opt_c: Treats the discretionary nature of the standard as a generalized-grievance bar
opt_d: Treats the case as nonjusticiable political question
explanation: Standing inquires into the plaintiff's harm, not the merits of the agency action. Discretionary balancing affects whether the agency is liable, not whether plaintiff has standing.

stem: A non-citizen detainee files a habeas petition challenging removal proceedings. Before the petition is decided, he is removed from the country. The case is:
opt_a: Live only if the removal violates international law
opt_b: Live because of collateral consequences (re-entry restrictions, future immigration consequences)
opt_c: Live only if he was in U.S. custody when the petition was filed
opt_d: Moot because he is no longer in U.S. custody
explanation: Spencer v. Kemna line: collateral consequences of removal (re-entry bars, immigration consequences) can keep habeas petitions live even after physical departure.

stem: A federal court is asked whether the Constitution permits the President to terminate a treaty without Senate approval. Under modern political-question doctrine:
opt_a: The question is reviewable only by the Senate
opt_b: The question is likely nonjusticiable because of textual commitment to political branches and lack of judicial standards
opt_c: The question is justiciable as a federal-law issue under § 1331
opt_d: The question is reviewable only after the President invokes the treaty's termination clause
explanation: Goldwater v. Carter (plurality): treaty-termination questions are political questions textually committed to political branches; this analysis informs modern foreign-relations cases.

stem: In Hayburn's Case, federal judges refused to act because the statute required them to certify pension claims subject to subsequent revision by the executive. The principle:
opt_a: Federal judges cannot review pension claims of any kind
opt_b: Federal judges cannot perform non-judicial functions whose results are subject to political-branch revision
opt_c: Federal judges may perform any function with party consent
opt_d: Federal judges cannot perform any function not expressly authorized by Article III
explanation: Hayburn's Case: the structural principle is that final adjudication is the essence of judicial power; subjecting judicial determinations to political revision destroys that essential attribute.

stem: In Ex parte McCardle, Congress repealed a specific habeas appellate jurisdiction during the case. McCardle held this valid. In Ex parte Yerger, the Court said:
opt_a: Congress could not strip jurisdiction over habeas after Yerger
opt_b: McCardle applied only to wartime jurisdiction
opt_c: McCardle was wrongly decided
opt_d: Other jurisdictional avenues (§ 14 original jurisdiction) remained, so McCardle's repeal didn't strip all habeas access
explanation: Yerger followed McCardle but emphasized that § 14 original-jurisdiction habeas remained. Congress hadn't stripped all habeas access.

stem: Justice Story's view of Article III, articulated in Martin v. Hunter's Lessee, holds that:
opt_a: The Supreme Court has inherent jurisdiction over state-law issues
opt_b: Article III does not authorize Supreme Court review of state-court judgments
opt_c: Article III's vesting clause is discretionary: Congress may decline to create federal courts
opt_d: Article III's vesting clause is mandatory: federal judicial power must vest somewhere in the federal courts
explanation: Story's mandatory-vesting view: Article III's 'shall be vested' is imperative. The Madisonian Compromise contemplated lower-court creation as discretionary, but the Court has rarely embraced Story's strong view.

stem: Crowell v. Benson required de novo Article III review of 'jurisdictional facts' that condition agency authority. Modern doctrine:
opt_a: Has expanded Crowell to require de novo review of all agency findings
opt_b: Has narrowed Crowell's specific requirement, but the broader principle that Article III review must accompany substantial agency adjudication endures
opt_c: Has formally overruled Crowell and rejected jurisdictional-fact review
opt_d: Has limited Crowell to admiralty cases
explanation: Crowell's specific 'jurisdictional fact' requirement has been narrowed in scope, but the broader principle of essential Article III review survives.

stem: In Stern v. Marshall, the Court held the bankruptcy court lacked authority to enter final judgment on a state-law tort counterclaim. The narrow exception:
opt_a: Common-law counterclaims that are necessarily resolved as part of claim allowance may be decided
opt_b: Common-law counterclaims may be decided if a creditor objects
opt_c: Common-law counterclaims may be decided if removed from state court
opt_d: All common-law counterclaims may be decided with party consent
explanation: Stern's narrow exception: counterclaims that are necessarily resolved by the claim-allowance process may be adjudicated by the bankruptcy court. (Wellness later allowed party consent more broadly.)

stem: Congress assigns adjudication of certain naturalization claims to an administrative tribunal. Under the public-rights doctrine:
opt_a: Naturalization is a public right Congress may assign to non-Article III adjudication
opt_b: Naturalization is a state-law matter outside federal-court jurisdiction
opt_c: Naturalization is a private right requiring Article III adjudication
opt_d: Naturalization is reserved exclusively to the federal courts under Article III
explanation: Naturalization is a public right entirely created by federal law; non-Article III adjudication is permissible under traditional public-rights doctrine.

stem: Under 28 U.S.C. § 157(d), the district court may withdraw the reference of a bankruptcy case from the bankruptcy court:
opt_a: Only after the bankruptcy court certifies the case as non-core
opt_b: Only with consent of all parties
opt_c: On its own motion or motion of a party, for cause shown
opt_d: Only at the request of the bankruptcy trustee
explanation: § 157(d): district courts may withdraw bankruptcy reference for cause; this is a key Article III safety valve preserved by Stern.

stem: Under Peretz v. United States, magistrates may conduct felony voir dire because:
opt_a: Defendants impliedly consent through participation, and the magistrate's role does not affect Article III's essential attributes
opt_b: Magistrates have inherent jurisdiction over jury selection
opt_c: Voir dire is a non-judicial function outside Article III
opt_d: Felony voir dire is a public-rights matter
explanation: Peretz: implied consent + non-essential nature of voir dire (district judge presides over the trial itself) preserved Article III's essence.

stem: Article III judges may be removed from office only:
opt_a: Through judicial-conduct proceedings under 28 U.S.C. § 351
opt_b: By impeachment for high crimes and misdemeanors, not by ordinary statutory mechanisms
opt_c: By Congressional vote of no confidence
opt_d: By the President with Senate confirmation of replacement
explanation: Article III judges hold office 'during good behavior' — removable only by impeachment under Article II.

stem: In Boumediene v. Bush, the Court held that the Detainee Treatment Act's substitute-review provisions were inadequate because:
opt_a: They were not retroactive to existing detainees
opt_b: They were limited to non-citizens captured outside the U.S.
opt_c: They were administrative rather than judicial
opt_d: They lacked sufficient safeguards (e.g., access to evidence, ability to develop record) to substitute for habeas review
explanation: Boumediene: a constitutionally adequate habeas substitute requires meaningful procedural safeguards (access to evidence, opportunity to develop record). The DTA's CSRT-plus-DC-Circuit review fell short.

stem: Plaintiff sues for a declaratory judgment that her conduct does not violate federal law, seeking to head off an anticipated enforcement action. Under Skelly Oil:
opt_a: FQJ exists if the federal-law issue would arise as a defense in the natural coercive action
opt_b: The DJ procedure waives the well-pleaded complaint rule
opt_c: FQJ exists if any federal-law issue is reasonably presented in the dispute
opt_d: FQJ is determined by the well-pleaded complaint that would have been filed in the natural coercive action
explanation: Skelly Oil extended Mottley to declaratory-judgment plaintiffs: the federal court asks what the well-pleaded complaint of the natural coercive action would look like.

stem: The Holmes rule of American Well Works states that:
opt_a: An action arises under the law that creates the cause of action
opt_b: An action arises under federal law if any federal element is implicated
opt_c: An action arises under federal law if removal would be available to either party
opt_d: An action arises under federal law if Congress has legislated on the subject
explanation: Holmes' rule: federal law creating the cause of action is sufficient (though not necessary, per Smith / Grable) for FQJ.

stem: Empire HealthChoice v. McVeigh refused to find FQJ over a state-law contractual subrogation claim that involved a federal benefits program because:
opt_a: The plaintiff failed to exhaust administrative remedies
opt_b: The federal benefits program had not yet been finalized
opt_c: The defendant was not a federal agency
opt_d: The federal-law issue was insufficiently substantial to warrant federal jurisdiction over the state-law claim
explanation: Empire HealthChoice: not all federal-law issues in state-law claims are 'substantial' under Grable. Background federal involvement is insufficient.

stem: § 1367(d) tolls the statute of limitations on supplemental state claims for:
opt_a: 6 months after dismissal
opt_b: 30 days after dismissal, or longer if state law provides a longer tolling period
opt_c: The full pendency of federal litigation
opt_d: Indefinitely until the plaintiff refiles in state court
explanation: § 1367(d): SOL tolled while supplemental claim is pending plus 30 days, unless state law provides longer. This protects plaintiffs whose state claims are dismissed late.

stem: Under § 1367(c), a federal court may decline supplemental jurisdiction when:
opt_a: The state-court venue is more convenient
opt_b: The state claim raises a novel or complex issue of state law, federal claims are dismissed early, or other compelling reasons exist
opt_c: The state claim involves a state agency
opt_d: The defendant requests dismissal
explanation: § 1367(c) lists four discretionary grounds for declining supplemental jurisdiction. Gibbs factors (economy/convenience/fairness/comity) inform the analysis.

stem: Under 28 U.S.C. § 1441(a), a defendant may remove a state-court action to federal court if:
opt_a: The plaintiff has consented in writing
opt_b: The defendant is sued in a state where she is not a citizen
opt_c: The federal court would have had original jurisdiction over the action
opt_d: The case is a class action
explanation: § 1441(a) permits removal of any action over which federal courts would have original jurisdiction. Specific exceptions and rules apply (e.g., § 1441(b) forum-defendant rule).

stem: For diversity-jurisdiction purposes, an American citizen domiciled abroad with no U.S. state citizenship is:
opt_a: Treated as a citizen of the District of Columbia
opt_b: Treated as a foreign citizen for alienage diversity
opt_c: Treated as a citizen of the state of his last U.S. domicile
opt_d: Generally treated as 'stateless' for diversity purposes, defeating diversity jurisdiction
explanation: Newman-Green v. Alfonzo-Larrain: a U.S. citizen domiciled abroad is 'stateless' for diversity, neither a state citizen nor a foreign national.

stem: For diversity-jurisdiction purposes, an LLC's citizenship is:
opt_a: Whichever state has the most members
opt_b: The citizenship of each of its members
opt_c: Its principal place of business, under Hertz
opt_d: The state of its formation, like a corporation
explanation: Carden v. Arkoma applied to LLCs: an unincorporated entity's citizenship traces to each of its members. Different rule from corporations.

stem: Under 28 U.S.C. § 1442, federal-officer removal:
opt_a: Permits federal officers (and persons acting under them) to remove state-court suits arising from on-duty conduct, even where the well-pleaded complaint contains no federal claim
opt_b: Requires the consent of all defendants in the action
opt_c: Is limited to suits against the United States
opt_d: Permits removal only when a federal-law defense is asserted
explanation: § 1442 federal-officer removal: federal officers may remove state-court suits arising from on-duty conduct, regardless of whether the complaint contains a federal claim. (Watson v. Philip Morris narrowed 'acting under' for private contractors.)

stem: CAFA generally permits removal of class actions with $5M aggregate amount-in-controversy and minimal diversity. Exceptions include:
opt_a: Class actions filed within 1 year of the underlying conduct
opt_b: Class actions involving state-law tort claims
opt_c: Local controversies (where two-thirds of class and primary defendants are forum-state citizens) and home-state controversies
opt_d: Class actions with fewer than 100 members
explanation: CAFA's local-controversy and home-state exceptions force certain locally-rooted class actions to remain in state court despite minimal diversity.

stem: A state court applies its long-standing 'preserved-issue' procedural rule to bar a federal claim raised for the first time in a motion for reconsideration. Under modern doctrine:
opt_a: The procedural ground is generally adequate to bar federal review if applied evenhandedly
opt_b: The procedural ground is preempted under Felder v. Casey
opt_c: The procedural ground is inadequate because it is novel
opt_d: The procedural ground is preempted because it bars federal claims
explanation: Walker v. Birmingham line: regularly-applied state procedural rules are 'adequate' to bar federal review, distinguishable from NAACP's novel applications.

stem: A state court explicitly invokes 'our state constitution's analog to the Fourth Amendment' as the basis for suppressing evidence. The Supreme Court reviewing this judgment should:
opt_a: Decline review because the state ground is adequate and independent
opt_b: Vacate and remand for clarification
opt_c: Apply Long's presumption and review on the merits
opt_d: Review only the federal-law portion of the opinion
explanation: Explicit invocation of an independent state ground that supports the result bars federal review under Fox Films/Long.

stem: Michigan v. Long's presumption applies when a state-court opinion appears to rest primarily on federal law and is not clearly independent. The presumption operates to:
opt_a: Bar federal review unless the state court explicitly invokes federal grounds
opt_b: Permit federal review unless the state court explicitly invokes adequate independent state grounds
opt_c: Reverse the burden of persuasion to the petitioner
opt_d: Require remand for clarification before deciding reviewability
explanation: Long: bright-line rule favoring federal review. The state court must explicitly invoke independent state grounds to bar federal review.

stem: In Capital Cities Media v. Toole, the Supreme Court vacated a state-court judgment and remanded for clarification. The procedure:
opt_a: Replaces Long's presumption when the basis is unclear
opt_b: Asks the state court to specify the basis for its decision so the Court can determine reviewability
opt_c: Categorically applies whenever federal-law issues are mentioned
opt_d: Is unavailable when the state court has already entered a final judgment
explanation: Capital Cities: vacate-and-remand for clarification is one of the Court's tools when an opinion is ambiguous. Long's presumption and Capital Cities remands are alternative responses.

stem: A state court holds that no contract was formed under state contract law, defeating a federal Contracts Clause claim. The Supreme Court considering review:
opt_a: Must defer absolutely to the state-court interpretation
opt_b: Disregards the state-law antecedent and addresses the federal claim as if a contract existed
opt_c: May examine the state-law contract question with deference to but not absolute fidelity
opt_d: Declines review because the state-law ground is adequate and independent
explanation: Indiana ex rel. Anderson v. Brand: where state-law antecedents determine federal rights, Supreme Court review is permitted with deference (but not absolute fidelity).

stem: A state court applies state-law absolute immunity to dismiss a § 1983 personal-capacity damages claim. Under Howlett v. Rose:
opt_a: The state-law immunity is preempted because § 1983 federal-law immunities govern
opt_b: The state-law immunity controls because § 1983 incorporates state-law immunities
opt_c: The plaintiff must refile in federal court
opt_d: The plaintiff's claim is barred by state sovereign immunity
explanation: Howlett v. Rose: state courts cannot use state-law immunities to defeat § 1983; federal-law immunities (e.g., Harlow QI) govern.

stem: Wisconsin requires § 1983 plaintiffs to give 120-day pre-suit notice; no analogous notice requirement applies to comparable state-law claims. Under Felder v. Casey:
opt_a: The state notice requirement applies in state court but not federal court
opt_b: The state notice requirement is preempted in § 1983 actions because it is outcome-determinative and burdens federal-law rights
opt_c: The state notice requirement applies because procedural rules are matters of state sovereignty
opt_d: The state notice requirement is permissible if it provides meaningful exhaustion
explanation: Felder v. Casey: state procedural rules that burden § 1983 enforcement and are outcome-determinative are preempted, even in state court.

stem: NAACP v. Alabama held that a state procedural ground was 'inadequate' to bar federal review because:
opt_a: The state had failed to publish the procedural rule
opt_b: The procedural rule was novel and had not been applied in analogous cases
opt_c: The procedural rule contradicted the state's own constitution
opt_d: The procedural rule was unconstitutional under federal law
explanation: NAACP v. Alabama: novel procedural applications — particularly when used against unpopular litigants — are not 'adequate' to bar federal review.

stem: A defendant raises a federal Confrontation Clause claim for the first time in his petition for state-supreme-court rehearing, having not raised it in the state intermediate court. The Supreme Court reviews:
opt_a: De novo because preservation rules vary among states
opt_b: Only if the state supreme court actually addressed the federal claim despite the late presentation
opt_c: Only with leave of the state attorney general
opt_d: Under plain-error principles
explanation: Cardinale: federal claims must generally be raised below. If the state supreme court actually addresses the federal claim despite belatedness, that may suffice for federal review.

stem: Murdock v. Memphis stands for the proposition that the Supreme Court's appellate review of state-court judgments:
opt_a: Includes review of state-law antecedent issues unless waived
opt_b: Extends to all errors in the state court's reasoning
opt_c: Is unavailable absent express congressional authorization
opt_d: Is generally limited to the federal-law questions in the case
explanation: Murdock established that the federal Supreme Court reviews only federal-law questions on appeal from state courts; state-law issues are not reviewable except where antecedent to a federal right (Brand).

stem: In a diversity action, the parties dispute whether the forum state's 'door-closing' statute (which limits standing for non-residents) applies. Under Erie / Hanna:
opt_a: The state statute does not apply because federal-court diversity jurisdiction overrides
opt_b: The state statute applies because all door-closing rules are procedural by classification
opt_c: The state statute applies only if formally adopted by the state supreme court
opt_d: The state statute likely applies if it is substantive and outcome-determinative; alternatively, if no FRCP rule is on point
explanation: Door-closing statutes (Woods v. Interstate Realty) raise classic Erie problems: state limits on access to courts are typically substantive for Erie purposes.

stem: FRCP 4 authorizes service of process by certain methods. State law in the forum state requires more elaborate service. The federal court applies:
opt_a: FRCP 4 because Hanna's two-track makes REA-authorized rules dispositive
opt_b: FRCP 4 only if state law is procedural rather than substantive
opt_c: Whichever method better protects defendant's due-process rights
opt_d: State service law because service is procedural
explanation: Hanna v. Plumer: REA-authorized FRCP rules apply over conflicting state law without further outcome-determinative analysis.

stem: Clearfield Trust's 'power' question asks whether a federal interest justifies displacing state law. The federal interest must be:
opt_a: Marginal but identifiable
opt_b: Constitutional rather than merely statutory
opt_c: Substantial enough that state law would actually frustrate federal policy
opt_d: Express in a federal statute
explanation: Clearfield's threshold: the federal interest must be substantial and the state law must actually threaten that interest. Miree v. DeKalb illustrates the limit.

stem: Boyle's three-part government-contractor defense applies to design-defect claims. Whether the defense extends to manufacturing-defect claims is:
opt_a: Determined by whether the contractor met state-tort manufacturing standards
opt_b: Settled by Boyle itself: the defense applies to all defective-product claims
opt_c: Settled: the defense does not extend to manufacturing defects
opt_d: Disputed; courts have not consistently extended Boyle beyond design defects
explanation: Lower courts have split on whether Boyle's design-defect rationale extends to manufacturing defects; the Supreme Court has not resolved this question.

stem: Under Kimbell Foods, federal courts borrow state law as the federal rule when:
opt_a: Congress has not occupied the field
opt_b: Uniformity is not essential to federal interests, and disrupting state-law commercial expectations would be counterproductive
opt_c: Federal common law is silent
opt_d: The state law is consistent with federal policy
explanation: Kimbell Foods's analysis: borrow state law where uniformity is not essential to federal interests, to avoid disrupting commercial expectations.

stem: In Banco Nacional v. Sabbatino, the Court held the act-of-state doctrine is federal common law because of:
opt_a: Article III's grant of jurisdiction over alienage cases
opt_b: The federal interest in foreign relations and the uniform treatment of foreign sovereigns
opt_c: International law's customary status as federal law
opt_d: Congressional authorization in the Hickenlooper Amendment
explanation: Sabbatino: act-of-state's character as federal common law derives from the federal interest in foreign relations, not from international law's status.

stem: § 301 of the LMRA gives federal courts jurisdiction over labor-contract suits but is silent on substantive rules. Lincoln Mills held:
opt_a: § 301 is unconstitutional because it lacks a substantive federal-law basis
opt_b: § 301 confers jurisdiction only; substantive law is governed by Erie
opt_c: § 301 incorporates the substantive law of the state where the contract was negotiated
opt_d: § 301 implicitly authorizes federal common law of labor contracts
explanation: Lincoln Mills: § 301's jurisdictional grant authorized federal common-law development. Frankfurter's dissent argued the grant was meaningless without substantive law and so unconstitutional.

stem: In Texas Industries v. Radcliff Materials, the Court declined to recognize a federal common-law right of contribution among antitrust violators because:
opt_a: Federal common law cannot create rights affecting private commercial conduct
opt_b: The Sherman Act preempted all state-law contribution rights
opt_c: Congress had crafted a comprehensive remedial scheme without including the right
opt_d: Antitrust contribution was a uniquely state-law matter
explanation: Texas Industries: federal common law is disfavored where Congress has crafted a comprehensive scheme without the contemplated right.

stem: Erie's twin aims under Hanna v. Plumer are:
opt_a: Discouraging forum-shopping and avoiding inequitable administration of laws between federal and state courts
opt_b: Preserving state sovereignty and federal supremacy
opt_c: Protecting jury rights and avoiding inconsistent verdicts
opt_d: Promoting judicial economy and respecting party autonomy
explanation: Hanna's framing: Erie aims to (1) prevent forum-shopping incentives that attract litigants to federal court and (2) avoid arbitrary outcome differences between federal and state courts.

stem: When federal common law is justified, the question of whether to fashion a uniform federal rule or borrow state law turns on:
opt_a: The number of states whose laws are implicated
opt_b: Congressional authorization for federal common-lawmaking
opt_c: The strength of the federal interest in uniformity weighed against disrupting commercial or state-law expectations
opt_d: Whether the state law is substantively identical to the proposed federal rule
explanation: Kimbell Foods's discretion question: courts weigh federal-interest uniformity against the disruption of commercial expectations from displacing settled state rules.

stem: Bivens v. Six Unknown Named Agents recognized an implied damages remedy for:
opt_a: Fourth Amendment violations by federal officers in the home
opt_b: Eighth Amendment violations in federal prisons
opt_c: Fifth Amendment due-process violations by federal officers
opt_d: First Amendment retaliation by federal supervisors
explanation: Bivens's narrow original holding: Fourth Amendment damages remedy for federal-officer warrantless search of a home.

stem: After Egbert v. Boule, a federal court considering whether to extend Bivens asks:
opt_a: Whether any rational reason exists for the political branches to handle the matter, in which case extension fails
opt_b: Whether alternative remedies are adequate substitutes for damages
opt_c: Whether special factors counsel hesitation under traditional common-law principles
opt_d: Whether the constitutional right is clearly established
explanation: Egbert collapsed the test: any rational reason for Congressional preference defeats Bivens extension.

stem: Davis v. Passman extended Bivens because the plaintiff:
opt_a: Was a NASA engineer demoted for whistleblowing speech
opt_b: Was a Congressional staffer fired on grounds of sex, and Title VII expressly excluded Congressional staff at the time
opt_c: Was a non-citizen detained at the border without process
opt_d: Was a federal-prison inmate denied medical care
explanation: Davis: Title VII's exclusion of Congressional staff left the plaintiff with no other federal remedy, justifying Bivens.

stem: Carlson v. Green held Bivens applied to an Eighth Amendment medical-care claim by a federal inmate, even though FTCA provided a remedy. The Court reasoned:
opt_a: FTCA does not permit suits arising in federal prisons
opt_b: Bivens damages directly against officers serve different functions than FTCA recovery (governed by state tort law)
opt_c: FTCA's exclusive-remedy provisions had not yet been enacted
opt_d: The plaintiff was suing for injunctive relief that the FTCA does not authorize
explanation: Carlson: Bivens and FTCA serve different purposes. Bivens provides uniform federal damages directly against officers; FTCA depends on state tort law.

stem: Cannon v. University of Chicago held Title IX includes an implied private right of action for:
opt_a: Intentional sex discrimination, given the rights-creating language and Congressional intent
opt_b: Disparate-impact sex discrimination, given the regulations
opt_c: Both intentional and disparate-impact claims, broadly construed
opt_d: Sex discrimination only where federal funds directly subsidize the discriminatory program
explanation: Cannon: Title IX implies PROA for intentional sex discrimination. Sandoval later cabined the PROA to intentional claims.

stem: Alabama administers driver's license tests only in English. A non-English-speaking applicant sues, alleging this disparately impacts national-origin minorities under Title VI's regulations. After Sandoval:
opt_a: Private parties may sue if the agency promulgating the regulations expressly authorized
opt_b: Private parties may sue because Title VI's regulations clearly intend private enforcement
opt_c: No private right of action exists for disparate-impact claims under Title VI's regulations
opt_d: The applicant's exclusive remedy is administrative complaint to DOJ
explanation: Sandoval: only Congress, not an agency through regulation, can create a private right of action; Title VI's text supports only intentional-discrimination PROAs.

stem: Cort v. Ash's four-factor test for implied private rights of action included:
opt_a: Standing, ripeness, mootness, and political-question avoidance
opt_b: Necessity, particularity, redressability, and substantial federal interest
opt_c: Express text, agency interpretation, history of enforcement, and congressional acquiescence
opt_d: Beneficiary class, legislative intent, fit with statutory purpose, and traditional state-law area
explanation: Cort v. Ash's classic four-factor analysis. Largely supplanted by Sandoval's text-and-structure approach.

stem: In Health & Hospital Corp. v. Talevski (2023), the Court held that Spending-Clause statutes can support § 1983 enforcement when:
opt_a: The text uses unambiguous rights-creating language and there is no comprehensive remedial scheme that displaces § 1983
opt_b: The state has accepted the federal funds at issue
opt_c: The statute creates a categorical entitlement to damages
opt_d: The agency administering the statute expressly authorizes private suits
explanation: Talevski: rights-creating language plus absence of an exclusive remedial scheme allows § 1983 enforcement.

stem: A U.S. Border Patrol agent shoots and kills a Mexican national across the border. The family sues under Bivens for Fourth and Fifth Amendment violations. After Hernandez v. Mesa:
opt_a: The family's exclusive remedy is diplomatic resolution through the State Department
opt_b: No Bivens action lies because cross-border shootings present a new context with foreign-relations special factors
opt_c: Bivens applies because the U.S. Constitution reaches federal officers' conduct extraterritorially
opt_d: Bivens applies for the Fourth Amendment but not the Fifth
explanation: Hernandez v. Mesa: cross-border context plus foreign-relations special factors defeat Bivens extension.

stem: Bush v. Lucas declined to recognize Bivens because:
opt_a: Federal employees implicitly waive Bivens by accepting government employment
opt_b: The federal civil-service remedial scheme provided adequate alternative review
opt_c: First Amendment retaliation is not a Bivens-cognizable harm
opt_d: The civil-service scheme provided full damages equivalent to Bivens
explanation: Bush v. Lucas: when Congress has crafted a comprehensive remedial scheme — even one that lacks compensatory damages — the implication is that further remedies are not warranted.

stem: Ex parte Young permits federal-court suits against state officers for:
opt_a: Both prospective and retroactive relief, with appropriate clear-statement abrogation
opt_b: Retroactive damages against the state treasury
opt_c: State-law violations where federal jurisdiction is otherwise proper
opt_d: Prospective injunctive relief to halt ongoing constitutional violations
explanation: Ex parte Young's narrow lane: prospective relief only. Edelman bars retroactive damages from the state treasury; Pennhurst bars state-law enforcement.

stem: A plaintiff successfully sues a state official under Ex parte Young and obtains an injunction. The injunction has the practical effect of compelling the state to pay $5M in back wages. Under Edelman v. Jordan:
opt_a: The injunction is permissible because it operates against the official, not the state directly
opt_b: The retroactive monetary aspect violates the 11th Amendment, even though styled as prospective compliance
opt_c: The injunction is permissible only if back wages exceed a de minimis threshold
opt_d: The injunction is permissible if § 1983 abrogates state immunity for back wages
explanation: Edelman v. Jordan: Ex parte Young permits prospective relief but bars retroactive monetary relief from the state treasury, even when styled as compliance.

stem: A class of disabled residents sues to compel state mental-health facilities to comply with state-law standards governing institutional conditions. Under Pennhurst v. Halderman:
opt_a: Federal courts may enforce state law against state officials whenever the underlying claim is constitutional
opt_b: The supremacy clause permits federal-court enforcement of state law in civil-rights cases
opt_c: No federal-court Ex parte Young suit lies because federal courts cannot use Young to enforce state law against state officials
opt_d: Federal courts have inherent jurisdiction to ensure state compliance with state law
explanation: Pennhurst v. Halderman: Ex parte Young is for federal-law enforcement; using it to enforce state law against state officials would intrude on state sovereignty.

stem: Under Seminole Tribe v. Florida, congressional abrogation of state sovereign immunity requires:
opt_a: Article I commerce-power authority and § 5 enforcement together
opt_b: An unequivocal expression of intent to abrogate AND exercise of a constitutional power that permits abrogation
opt_c: An unequivocal expression of intent and a state's express consent
opt_d: A federal interest sufficient to displace state sovereign immunity
explanation: Seminole Tribe's two-step. Article I commerce powers are insufficient; § 5 of the 14th Amendment works.

stem: An employee sues a state university under the Family and Medical Leave Act's family-care provision. Under Nevada Department of Human Resources v. Hibbs:
opt_a: The employee's exclusive remedy is administrative review under state employment law
opt_b: The FMLA's family-care provision is a valid § 5 abrogation because it remedies historical sex discrimination in family leave
opt_c: The FMLA's abrogation is invalid because it lacks 'congruence and proportionality'
opt_d: The FMLA cannot abrogate state immunity because it is grounded in commerce power
explanation: Hibbs: FMLA's family-care provision was upheld as valid § 5 abrogation, citing the history of sex discrimination Congress sought to remedy.

stem: A paraplegic plaintiff sues a state under Title II of the ADA, alleging the state's courthouse lacks accessibility. Under Tennessee v. Lane:
opt_a: The plaintiff's exclusive remedy is administrative complaint to DOJ
opt_b: Title II's abrogation is invalid because it exceeds § 5 'congruence and proportionality'
opt_c: Title II's abrogation is valid as applied because access to courts implicates fundamental rights
opt_d: Title II applies only to private accommodations, not state courthouses
explanation: Tennessee v. Lane: Title II abrogation is valid as applied to fundamental-rights cases like access to courts.

stem: Central Virginia Community College v. Katz upheld bankruptcy proceedings against state interests because:
opt_a: Congress validly abrogated SSI under § 5 of the 14th Amendment
opt_b: The 11th Amendment does not address Article I bankruptcy
opt_c: States consented to federal bankruptcy jurisdiction in the plan of the constitutional convention
opt_d: Bankruptcy is a public-rights matter outside state immunity
explanation: Katz: ratification of the Bankruptcy Clause implicitly waived state immunity for bankruptcy proceedings — the 'plan of convention' theory.

stem: Torres v. Texas Department of Public Safety extended the plan-of-convention reasoning to:
opt_a: Suits enforcing federal employment protections under congressional war powers
opt_b: Suits enforcing the Equal Protection Clause directly
opt_c: Suits enforcing federal admiralty law against state actors
opt_d: Suits enforcing the Spending Clause as applied to states
explanation: Torres held that states yielded sovereign immunity for war-power-based federal claims (USERRA) under the plan of convention.

stem: Monroe v. Pape held that § 1983 reaches:
opt_a: Only state officials acting within the scope of their authority
opt_b: State officials acting under color of state law, even when violating state law
opt_c: Federal officials acting jointly with state officials
opt_d: Only state officials in their official capacity
explanation: Monroe interpreted 'under color of' broadly to reach abuses of authority — the heart of § 1983 enforcement against rogue officers.

stem: A county prosecutor authorizes a particular police entry into a doctor's office; the entry is later held unconstitutional. Under Pembaur v. Cincinnati:
opt_a: The county is shielded by sovereign immunity for individual prosecutorial decisions
opt_b: Only the prosecutor personally is liable; municipal liability does not attach
opt_c: The single decision constitutes county 'policy' if the prosecutor was the final policymaker on the issue
opt_d: The county is liable only if a pattern of similar decisions exists
explanation: Pembaur: a single decision by a final policymaker can constitute Monell 'policy' for § 1983 municipal liability.

stem: Williams v. Taylor distinguished § 2254(d)(1)'s 'contrary to' from 'unreasonable application' as follows:
opt_a: 'Contrary to' applies when the state court reached a conclusion opposite to clearly established law on materially indistinguishable facts; 'unreasonable application' applies when the state court correctly identified the rule but applied it unreasonably
opt_b: 'Contrary to' is reviewed de novo; 'unreasonable application' is reviewed for clear error
opt_c: The two prongs are functionally identical and should be treated as one
opt_d: 'Contrary to' applies to facts; 'unreasonable application' applies to law
explanation: Williams' canonical articulation. 'Contrary to' = wrong rule or opposite outcome on indistinguishable facts. 'Unreasonable application' = right rule, unreasonable application.

stem: Cullen v. Pinholster restricted federal habeas review under § 2254(d)(1) to:
opt_a: Any record developed in subsequent state collateral review
opt_b: The record before the state court at the time of its merits adjudication
opt_c: The federal-habeas record, including evidence developed in federal court
opt_d: The combined state and federal records, with deference to state findings
explanation: Pinholster: when § 2254(d)(1) applies, federal review is limited to the state-court record. Discovery on AEDPA review is sharply curtailed.

stem: A defendant fails to object at trial to allegedly coerced confession evidence; the state appellate court denies the claim under a state-procedural-default rule. The federal habeas court should:
opt_a: Defer to the state-court procedural ruling without further inquiry
opt_b: Apply AEDPA's § 2254(d)(1) standard to the procedural default itself
opt_c: Reach the merits because federal courts review federal claims de novo
opt_d: Apply the cause-and-prejudice standard to determine whether to excuse the procedural default
explanation: Wainwright v. Sykes: cause-and-prejudice (or actual-innocence gateway) is the standard for excusing procedural default.

stem: A federal habeas petitioner argues that his collateral-review counsel was ineffective for failing to raise an IATC claim. Under Coleman v. Thompson:
opt_a: Collateral-review attorney error excuses default if the underlying claim is colorable
opt_b: Collateral-review counsel's negligence triggers a Strickland-style federal habeas analysis
opt_c: Collateral-review attorney error is always 'cause' under federal due-process standards
opt_d: Collateral-review attorney error is generally not 'cause' to excuse default, with a narrow Martinez exception
explanation: Coleman: no constitutional right to collateral-review counsel means error there isn't 'cause' (Martinez carved a narrow exception).

stem: In an Arizona-style state where IATC claims must be raised first on initial-review collateral, the petitioner's collateral-review counsel was ineffective. Under Martinez v. Ryan:
opt_a: The petitioner must independently establish actual innocence to excuse default
opt_b: The procedural default is automatically excused because no other forum was available
opt_c: Ineffective collateral-review counsel is never 'cause' under Coleman v. Thompson
opt_d: Ineffective collateral-review counsel may serve as 'cause' to excuse default of the substantial IATC claim
explanation: Martinez v. Ryan: where state law channels IATC to initial-review collateral, ineffective counsel there can be 'cause' for the IATC default.

stem: After a state conviction becomes final, the U.S. Supreme Court announces a new constitutional rule of criminal procedure regarding jury selection. Under Teague v. Lane:
opt_a: The new rule applies on collateral review only to capital cases
opt_b: The new rule applies on collateral review because federal courts apply current constitutional law
opt_c: The new rule generally does not apply on collateral review unless it falls within the substantive-rule or watershed-procedural exceptions
opt_d: The new rule applies on collateral review only if the state expressly opts in
explanation: Teague: new procedural rules don't apply retroactively on collateral review except for narrow exceptions.

stem: A state prisoner whose Fourth Amendment exclusionary-rule claim was fully litigated in state court (suppression hearing, appellate review) seeks federal habeas relief. Under Stone v. Powell:
opt_a: Federal habeas relief is unavailable because the state provided full and fair litigation opportunity
opt_b: Federal habeas relief is available only if the petitioner is actually innocent
opt_c: Federal habeas review proceeds under § 2254(d)(1) deference
opt_d: Federal habeas review proceeds de novo because Fourth Amendment claims are core
explanation: Stone v. Powell: 4A exclusionary-rule claims are not cognizable on habeas where the state gave full and fair litigation opportunity.

stem: A petitioner files federal habeas more than one year after conviction-finality but produces credible new affidavits showing actual innocence. Under McQuiggin v. Perkins:
opt_a: The petitioner must produce DNA evidence to invoke the actual-innocence gateway
opt_b: Actual innocence excuses procedural default but not the AEDPA SOL
opt_c: The actual-innocence showing equitably tolls AEDPA's one-year statute of limitations
opt_d: The petition must be dismissed because AEDPA's SOL is jurisdictional
explanation: McQuiggin: a credible actual-innocence claim under Schlup serves as an equitable gateway through AEDPA's SOL.

stem: In Boumediene v. Bush, the Court held that:
opt_a: Habeas is unavailable to non-citizens
opt_b: The Suspension Clause limits Congress's ability to strip habeas jurisdiction over Guantanamo detainees
opt_c: The Suspension Clause has no extraterritorial reach
opt_d: The Detainee Treatment Act provides an adequate substitute for habeas in all cases
explanation: Boumediene held that Guantanamo detainees enjoy Suspension Clause-protected habeas, and the MCA's substitute review was inadequate.

stem: A state prisoner files a federal habeas petition without first presenting his federal claim to the state's highest court. The federal court should:
opt_a: Dismiss with prejudice because exhaustion is jurisdictional
opt_b: Reach the merits because federal habeas is independent of state-court review
opt_c: Dismiss for failure to exhaust state remedies, with leave to refile after exhaustion
opt_d: Stay proceedings to allow exhaustion in parallel state proceedings
explanation: § 2254(b) requires exhaustion of state remedies; failure to exhaust leads to dismissal without prejudice (or stay-and-abeyance in mixed-petition situations).
